data.title: Decomposition Of U.S. Equity Returns Over Time - Is It Different This Time?
data.description:
By Indrani De, CFA, PRM, Head of Global Investment Research, Americas, FTSE Russell | Indhu Raghavan, CFA, Manager, Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell

US equity return decomposition shows that the large US equity rallies in 2023 and 2024 were driven mainly by valuation expansion, unlike what we have seen during market recoveries in previous business cycles when, after an initial bounce in valuation, earnings growth was the main driver of total return. The analysis also highlights a structural shift post-2010, when US equities hugely outperformed global peers, where valuation changes played a far bigger role in US equity returns than during 2003-10. This suggests that US equity market returns could be more susceptible to changes in investor sentiment and that investors could potentially face greater equity market volatility going forward.

The US stock market recorded a second year of stellar double-digit growth in 2024. What can investors expect going forward?

A lot depends on the drivers of an equity rally, given that equity returns are driven by three main components - earnings growth, valuation changes and dividend yield. We analyze time series data of the valuation change, earnings growth and dividend yield components of US equity returns over the last 22 years. We look at how their relative contributions have evolved over time, and the pattern of these components at similar points in the economic cycle (e.g. years after recession, or recession fears on the back of monetary tightening.) This helps to think about the sustainability of these returns and the risk factors involved.

Exhibit 1 shows the annual total return for the US large-cap Russell 1000 index with a breakdown of contributions to that total return from price-to-earnings (PE) multiple expansion, earnings growth and dividend yield.

Methodology: We take the price index and IBES 12-month forward PE at any given point in time to calculate the implied earnings per share for the index. We use the values from 31 December of each year for PE and EPS to then calculate each component's growth rate from the year before. The dividend yield is calculated as the difference between index total return and price return for that year.

Exhibit 1: Russell 1000 total return decomposition, 12M, USD, Percent

The Russell 1000 index returned 26.5% and 24.5% in 2023 and 2024, respectively. Of the 26.5% total return in 2023, 19.4% pts (or nearly three-quarters) came from valuation expansion and 5.4% pts (or just one-fifth) from earnings growth. In 2024, 12.5% pts (or just over half of total return) came from valuation expansion and 10.5% pts (just over two-fifth) from earnings growth. The stock rally of the last two years also followed a massive pullback in equities in 2022 (-19.1% total return for Russell 1000), when the US Fed started aggressively tightening monetary conditions in response to spiralling inflation, sparking fears of a recession, and valuations contracted massively. In 2022, PE multiple contraction shaved off 24.5% pts from total return, while earnings growth of 4.0% and dividend yield of 1.4% softened the extent of the drawdown.

While the US avoided a recession during the subsequent two years after the monetary tightening that started in 2022, earnings growth was not the main driver of returns. How does this compare to history?

Is the current rally different from those in previous business cycles?

It is worth looking at two time periods when the US stock market recovered from sharp pullbacks-after the bursting of the Tech bubble during 2000-02[1] and the Global Financial Crisis during 2008-09.

During 2003-05, earnings growth contributed more to stock market returns than multiple expansion. In fact, multiples bounced in 2003 and then contracted subsequently in 2004 and 2005. In 2003, a total return of 29.9% was driven by 18.0% pts (or nearly two-thirds) of earnings growth, 9.8% pts of valuation expansion and 2.1% pts in dividend yield. In 2004 and 2005, earnings growth contributed 15.9% pts and 12.9% pts, respectively, to total return, which was about two-thirds and just over half of that return. At the same time, multiples contracted in 2004 and 2005.

We see a similar recovery during 2009-11 when multiples bounced back sharply in 2009, and earnings growth led stock market returns in the subsequent two years. In 2009, a total return of 28.4% was driven entirely by valuation expansion of 29.8% pts, while earnings contracted by 4.0% pts and dividend yield contributed 2.6% pts. In 2010 and 2011, earnings growth contributed 23.5% pts and 12.6% pts, respectively, to total return, which was about two-thirds and just under half of that return. Once again, multiples contracted during 2010-11.

The stock market recovery of 2023-24 seemed to follow a similar pattern initially-with a strong bounce back in multiples in 2023 and a more gradual recovery in earnings. Thereafter, in 2024, things looked different this time. It is hard to miss that the level of earnings growth this time around is much lower, and multiple expansion continued to contribute just over half of returns even in 2024.

These numbers may reflect both the narrowness of the US equity rally in the most recent period and the degree to which it was driven by sentiment. US returns have been dominated by the US Technology industry on the back of AI-fuelled optimism.

We do the same analysis for small caps to see if things look different for them relative to large caps. Looking at the small-cap space offers another perspective.

Exhibit 2 shows the annual total return for the Russell 2000 index with the breakdown of contributions from multiple expansion, earnings growth and dividend yield.

Exhibit 2: Russell 2000 total return decomposition, 12M, USD, Percent

The Russell 2000 small-cap index returned 16.9% and 11.5% over 2023 and 2024, respectively. This growth came primarily from multiple expansion of 19.7% pts and 8.2% pts, respectively, in 2023 and 2024, or over two-thirds of total return in both years. The contrast of this breakdown with the previous two time periods is starker. We see that during the 2003-05 recovery, it was earnings growth that dominated stock market returns in all the three years, and during 2009-11, earnings growth led the way in 2010 and 2011. The results for Russell 2000 are consistent with or even stronger than Russell 1000, i.e. market recoveries began with valuation expansion to be quickly followed by earnings growth as the main driver of returns, and the pattern of returns in 2023-24, of being led primarily by valuation expansion, is starkly different from past cycles.

US small caps are much more sensitive to domestic economic and monetary conditions than large caps. And small-cap earnings have recovered much less so than in the large-cap space and relative to history. It reinforces the idea that the stock market growth we have seen over the last two years in the US has been carried more by positive sentiment-led valuation expansion than a recovery in earnings compared to past cycles.

The expanding role of valuation expansion

In fact, if we look at the 14 years after the height of the European debt crisis in 2010, when US equity outperformance of global peers began in earnest,[2] we see that valuation expansion has played an increasing role in driving total returns in the US during 2011-24. The Russell 1000 index recorded a positive total return in 12 of those 14 years. Multiple expansion dominated returns in seven out of those 12 years, contributing between 50% and 90% of total return. By contrast, during 2003-10, when Russell 1000 had positive total returns in seven out of the eight years, only in one of those eight positive-return years was valuation expansion the primary driver for total returns.

Exhibit 3 shows the annualised total return for the Russell 1000 index during 2011-24 and 2003-10 and the contribution of each equity return component. We see that earnings growth contributed 7.9% pts and 7.5% pts to total return during 2011-24 and 2003-10, respectively. However, while multiples contracted during 2003-10 shaving off 2.3% pts from total return, multiple expansion contributed 3.8% pts (or nearly one-third) to total return during 2011-24. Simply put, annualized returns during 2011-24 were nearly double that during 2003-10, while earnings growth during 2011-24 was only marginally higher than the earlier period. Dividend yield, which tends to be more stable, was similar during both periods.

Exhibit 3: Annualized total return and contribution of valuation expansion, earnings growth and dividend yield, Percent

Period Ann. Total Return PE EPS DY
2011-24 13.6% 3.8% 7.9% 2.0%
2003-10 7.2% -2.3% 7.5% 2.0%

Source: FTSE Russell and LSEG. Data as of 31 December 2024. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Note: The total return for each year is indicated in the box. PE, EPS and DY show the valuation expansion, earnings growth and dividend yield components of total return, respectively.

This shows that since 2010, valuation expansion has played a greater role in US equity performance than previously. In that context, the contribution of multiple expansion to total return over the last two years is not an anomaly. What is an anomaly is that compared to the recovery years post-recession in a business cycle, the market recovery over the last two years were different, where the rallies were still driven primarily by valuation expansion and not earnings growth. This anomaly could potentially point to correction risks in US equities.

Conclusion

The large US equity rallies in 2023 and 2024 have been driven mainly by valuation expansion. In one sense that is a continuation of the structural trend in recent history, post the European debt crisis, when valuation expansion has played a larger role in driving US equity returns than previously. Yet it is different from cyclical trends, where post-recession (or recession fear years) where the initial rally is driven by valuation expansion, but earnings growth picks up the baton very soon. In the market rally after the 2022 drawdown, valuation expansion has yet to turn the baton over to earnings growth as the main driver.

Given this predominance of multiple expansion as a driver of equity returns over the past two years, it suggests that US equity market fortunes may be more susceptible to changes in investor sentiment, particularly as it relates to the AI-fuelled tech rally. We saw an example of this in January 2025, when the release of the Chinese DeepSeek AI model, that was reportedly trained at a fraction of the cost of similar purpose US AI models, brought the tech rally to a halt. Tech stocks in the large-cap index returned zero in aggregate over January 2025. Technology was the worst performing equity industry (based on ICB industries) and US equities lagged developed market peers in Europe, the UK and Asia Pacific in January 2025. It also implies that investors could potentially face greater equity market volatility going forward.

1. We start our analysis in 2003 due to the availability of component data for index total return. The Russell 1000 index recorded negative total return in calendar years 2000 (-7.8%), 2001 (-12.4%) and 2002 (-21.7%), making 2003 the first calendar year of positive market returns after the bursting of the Tech Bubble.

2. As measured by the cumulative total returns of the FTSE USA index (507%) and the FTSE All-World ex USA index (95%) between end-2010 and end-2024.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: Share Price And Fundamentals Continue To Diverge
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks' (NASDAQ:PANW) share price continues to move higher, driven predominantly by multiple expansion. There is little to warrant this from a fundamental perspective though, as Palo Alto's growth remains modest, and its cash flow margins are coming under pressure. While the hardware part of the business is recovering from a weak period between late 2023 and late 2024, growth across the rest of the business continues to moderate.

I previously suggested that there was little reason to own Palo Alto's stock given the high valuation, floundering growth and cash flows that were not reflective of the company's underlying profitability. I continue to believe that Palo Alto will generate modest returns for shareholders going forward, despite SASE, cloud security and SIEM still offering large growth opportunities.

Market Conditions

Palo Alto suggested that cybersecurity demand was solid in the second quarter and is expected to remain so going forward. Firewall hardware demand certainly seems to have improved, and cloud security, SASE and SIEM remain growth drivers. Despite this, I think it is unlikely that Palo Alto's revenue growth will accelerate materially from current levels.

Even if the overall demand environment remains sluggish, there are a number of trends that should be supportive of Palo Alto's business, including vendor consolidation and AI. Gartner believes that 75% of security leaders are pursuing a consolidation strategy, although only around 15% of large enterprises have actually made progress in this area. By 2028, 45% of organizations are expected to use fewer than 15 cybersecurity tools, up from 13% in 2023. While it is not clear that Palo Alto has a significant lead in any one area, the company's product portfolio is probably the strongest as a whole.

AI is contributing to a resurgence of cloud transformation projects, which is supporting network security demand and is currently a tailwind for Palo Alto's firewall business. Software firewalls deployed in public clouds now represent around 70% of Palo Alto’s total virtual firewall ARR. Data security is also an early beneficiary of AI, with Palo Alto acquiring Dig for its data security posture management solution in an attempt to capitalize on this. Cybersecurity is competing with AI for IT budget though, which could be offsetting any tailwinds.

Improving hardware demand is also supportive of Palo Alto's business, particularly given that the company's hardware firewall market share has been increasing by around 2-3% annually. This situation is expected to continue and could be aided by Fortinet’s (FTNT) upcoming renewal cycle. Roughly 25% of Fortinet’s hardware firewall customer base is up for renewal in 2026. It will be difficult for Palo Alto to capture any of Fortinet's market share given the strength of Fortinet's products though. Palo Alto continues to expect 0-5% appliance market growth, making this part of the market more important for strategic reasons than potential growth.

Palo Alto Business Updates

Network Security continues to dominate Palo Alto's business, contributing around 80% of bookings. This part of the business is being driven by strong software and SASE demand, along with a rebound in firewall hardware demand. Firewall-as-a-Platform bookings increased 21% YoY in the second quarter, supported by stable hardware demand and market share gains. Software and SASE make up roughly two thirds of Palo Alto's firewall bookings and are growing significantly faster than the rest of the firewall business. Software firewalls have also been an area of strength recently, with bookings growing 50% YoY in the quarter, driven by AI and public cloud adoption. Approximately 70% of Palo Alto's VM deployments are now in the public cloud.

SASE customers were up over 20% in the second quarter to 5,600, while bookings growth exceed 50%, driven by larger deals. Palo Alto is also having greater success selling outside of its existing customer base, with 40% of new SASE customers in the first quarter new to Palo Alto. Recent strength has been supported by newer modules, like Autonomous Digital Experience Management, Cloud Access Security Broker, Prisma Access Browser and AI access.

The browser has become more of a focus for cybersecurity vendors in recent years and Palo Alto acquired Talon to strengthen its capabilities in this area. Adoption of the Prisma Access Browser is rising, with over 115 new customers and 1 million licenses added since the Talon acquisition. Prisma Access currently has around 16 million active licenses.

Generative AI has also made (DSPM) Data Security Posture Management a focus area, with Palo Alto acquiring Dig to strengthen this part of its business. Dig has now been integrated, allowing customers to determine what data is at risk, prioritize their response and remediate automatically. Palo Alto also offers Secure AI by Design (AI runtime and AI-SPM) in the cloud and early traction has been promising.

Palo Alto also believes that real-time cloud security is becoming more important. In support of this, Palo Alto has merged its Prisma Cloud platform with its CDR capabilities and Cortex to create Cortex Cloud. Cortex and Prisma Cloud bookings were up roughly 50% in the second quarter.

Cortex customers grew 20% YoY in the quarter, with Palo Alto signing hundreds of new XDR customers that are opportunities for SOC transformations in the future. QRadar added over 550 SaaS customers to Palo Alto’s Cortex customer base though. QRadar related bookings also provided an additional 100 million USD boost to Cortex in the second quarter.

The SIEM replacement opportunity is perhaps the most exciting part of Palo Alto's business at the moment. The company has already achieved significant success with its XSIAM solution and recent Rapid7 (RPD) and CrowdStrike (CRWD) earnings suggest the replacement opportunity is growing. Palo Alto acquired QRadar (IBM’s SIEM business) in August to accelerate the growth of its SIEM solution. It has been working with IBM to migrate as many customers as possible. Palo Alto had 150 active XSIAM customers at the end of the first quarter, with 40 of those having more than 1 million USD in ARR. Palo Alto expects to convert around half of the QRadar customers to XSIAM this year.

Palo Alto also continues to focus on driving adoption of its entire platform. The company added over 70 platformizations in the first quarter, with around a third of those coming from its acquisition of QRadar SaaS, giving it a total of approximately 1,100). Palo Alto added a further 75 platformizations in Q2. The company is targeting 2,500-3,500 by FY2030, which will be an important part of reaching 15 billion USD ARR.

Financial Analysis

Palo Alto’s revenue totaled 2.26 billion USD in the second quarter, an increase of 14% YoY, with 8% product revenue growth and 16% services revenue growth. Close to 40% of product revenue now comes from software. Within services revenue, subscription revenue grew 20% and support revenue was up 8%.

RPO totaled 13 billion USD at the end of Q2, up 21% YoY, with current RPO increasing 17% to 6.1 billion USD. NGS ARR increased 37% to 4.78 billion USD driven by SASE and Cortex.

Palo Alto's revenue growth appears set to reaccelerate in the second half of FY2025, although I tend to think that growth will stabilize in the mid-teens rather than continuing to move higher.

Palo Alto's net new NGS ARR has actually been fairly weak in the past few quarters, outside of the QRadar deal. For example, Palo Alto added 230 million USD ARR in the first quarter, versus 270 million USD a year earlier, with QRadar contributing 74 million USD ARR. It is worth noting that FY2024 benefitted from the transition of old attaches to cloud-delivered advanced subscriptions though. Even so, Palo Alto's net new ARR has now been flat for some time.

Palo Alto’s gross margin declined to 76.6% in the second quarter, which was attributed to the growth of newer SaaS offerings that are yet to achieve scale. There were also some one-time costs expenses related to inventory and product transitions that were higher than usual.

In addition to operating leverage, Palo Alto is leveraging AI across its business to improve its operating margins. For example, Palo Alto is on track to reduce its use of IT contractors by around 50%. The company is also utilizing an internally developed copilot to assist with customer support. The copilot is being used in around 85% of network security cases, resulting in roughly a 50% reduction in resolution time. Palo Alto is also deploying copilot tools for its developers and early results are reportedly promising.

Palo Alto's cash flows remain under pressure though due to more favorable payment plans and vendor financing. The percentage of bookings on deferred payment plans increased from 6% in FY20 to 30% in the first quarter. Palo Alto has also been offering a range of incentives, like discounting and trial periods.

Conclusion

There are a number of factors that are supportive of Palo Alto, including improved firewall hardware demand, SASE, cloud security and the legacy SIEM replacement opportunity. Vendor consolidation is also an important tailwind, which Palo Alto is trying to capitalize on with its platformization strategy. While each of the main cybersecurity vendors has its own strengths and weaknesses, there are a relatively small number of companies that are emerging as winners in the consolidation process, and Palo Alto is perhaps the best positioned.

Palo Alto's valuation means that forward returns are likely to be fairly poor though. Adjusting for a broad-based growth slowdown, SaaS valuations now appear to be in line with the euphoric peaks of 2021. While this situation could persist for some time, it seems likely to break down at some point, particularly if companies cannot follow through on rising expectations.

Palo Alto recognizes that its stock is expensive, choosing not to repurchase any shares in the second quarter even though it has cash and a 1 billion USD authorization remaining through December 2025. Palo Alto's share price got as low as 168 USD in the quarter and the company still did not repurchase any shares. From a valuation perspective, I tend to think that Palo Alto would look a lot more attractive at around 110 USD per share. A recession or significant headwinds would likely be necessary for this to occur though.

data.title: Eagle Point Credit: Q4 Earnings Indicate Both Strength And Weakness (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Eagle Point Credit (NYSE:ECC) operates as a closed end fund that aims to generate attractive totals returns through its portfolio of CLO (collateralized loan obligation) equity and debt investments. CLOs often come with an elevated level of risk, but investors are rewarded with a much higher level of cash flow because of the higher dividend yield offered. ECC currently offers a dividend yield of 21.5%, which may seem suspiciously high but is fully covered by earnings at the moment. I previously covered ECC in mid 2024 and thought it would be a good time to revisit the fund since their Q4 earnings were recently released.

Looking at the performance of ECC over the last twelve months, we can see that the price has fallen by about 13.2%. When including all of the dividends paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 6.8% over the same period. One of the main appeals of ECC would be the high rate of income that investors can receive from its monthly distributions. However, I am unpleased with the level of capital loss that has been experienced here. The overall NAV growth leaves a lot to be desired and can certainly be improved. Unfortunately, I believe that ECC's price stability is directly related to the interest rate environment that we remain in.

When I previously covered ECC, I issued a buy rating because the outlook at the time was that there'd be several interest rate cuts to take place. Interest rate cuts would have a positive effect on ECC's portfolio of floating rate debt, essentially providing borrowers with a reduced cost of debt and making it easier for them to see positive operating margins. However, the outlook for interest rate cuts has drastically changed since the last time I covered ECC and may make it much more difficult for ECC to see some positive price momentum. So let's first start by taking a look at ECC's portfolio strategy and how it has changed over the last few quarters.

Portfolio Strategy

CLOs are essentially different loans that have been bundled together and then broken down into different risk levels, known as tranches. What gives CLOs the reputation of being more risky is that the bundle of loans typically consists of borrowers that have below investment grade credit ratings. So these may be borrowers with less consistent cash flows or weaker balance sheets that may be more vulnerable and succumb to headwinds they face.

According to the most recent portfolio breakdown, ECC has net assets amounting to approximately $1.1B spread across 238 different CLO investments. We can see that 78.8% of their portfolio is focused on CLO Equity investments. Investors must be aware that CLO equity sits on the bottom of the corporate capital structure, which means it has the lowest priority for repayment. Therefore, CLO Equity investments are typically considered to be the riskiest, since that's where all of the losses are absorbed. Conversely, CLO debt investments sit higher on the capital structure and present less risk since these investments have a higher priority for repayment. The main difference between ECC and its sibling fund, Eagle Point Income (EIC), is that ECC focuses mainly on CLO Equity while EIC focuses more on CLO Debt.

ECC's portfolio is extremely diverse as it has exposure to 1,895 underlying obligors. The top ten obligors only account for 4.77% of the portfolio as well. Looking at the industry breakdown, we can see that technology software services account for the largest slice of the portfolio at 11.6%. This is followed by health care investments at 5.4% and hotels, restaurants, and leisure at 5%. The combination of these three industries makes up about 22% of the portfolio, so we can assume that these will have the greatest influence on ECC's success.

Additionally, 95.9% of the portfolio's CLO debt investments are structured on a floating rate basis and classified as senior secured loans. The floating rate structure means that ECC has been able to collect a higher amount of interest income from its underlying borrowers. This is because a higher interest rate is directly correlated to a higher interest expense that obligors have to pay. While this may be great for income collection, we've remained in a higher interest rate environment for about two years now, and it can put strain on underlying borrowers.

Q4 Earnings & Risk Profile

ECC reported their Q4 earnings and the results were a bit mixed. Most notably, net investment income landed at $0.24 per share, which missed estimates by $0.04. This can be partly attributed to higher realized losses for the quarter, totaling $0.12 per share. This was an increase from the prior quarter's net realized losses of $0.06 per share. As a result of the losses experienced, ECC's NAV has been on a downtrend over the last few quarters. Unfortunately, I expect the NAV to continue to deteriorate in the short term as the portfolio is more likely to experience defaults as a result of higher rates. For reference, here are the NAV amounts over the last few quarters:

  • Q4 2023: $9.21
  • Q1 2024: $9.16
  • Q2 2024: $8.75
  • Q3 2024: $8.44
  • Q4 2024: $8.38

Despite the NAV decreasing, recurring portfolio cash distributions received amounted to $0.74 per share. This remains consistent with the range of prior quarters and is an indication that the portfolio continues to generate a high amount of income. The investment income from CLO equity has actually increased to $37.27 per share, up from the prior quarter's total of $33.69 per share. Including all other income channels from the portfolio, total gross income landed at $49.54 per share, which continues the uptrend over the last twelve months. Something to note is that the total amount of assets continues to increase over time. Assets now sit at $1.1B, which is a sizable increase from the prior quarter's assets totaling $929M.

Management continues to prioritize allocating capital towards new investments, which can offset the short term headwinds caused by higher rates. For instance, $223.5M was deployed into new investments at a weighted average yield of 17.8%. As long as new investments continue to be made, I believe that ECC will be able to effectively grow NAV when conditions improve. Unfortunately, we have no accurate way to gauge when interest rate cuts will happen.

Remember, ECC's portfolio is primarily composed of below investment grade investments. Estimates from CEF Data indicate that about 69% of their portfolio is exposed to B-rated investments. For reference, anything rated below BBB is considered to be below investment grade. Looking at the price of ECC in relation to the federal funds rate reveals a clear inverse relationship. When rates were cut to near zero levels following the pandemic in 2020, ECC's price rapidly moved to the upside since it was easier for underlying borrowers to grow valuations with strategic use of cheap debt.

Conversely, the price has continued to deteriorate as rates remain near their decade high. When I previously covered ECC, the Fed stated that they estimated several rate cuts to take place over 2024 and 2025. Now that we've crossed into 2025, the outlook has shifted to a more hawkish stance. The Fed originally estimated that there would be four rate cuts to take place over this year but because of stubborn inflationary data, the estimate has now been cut down to only 1 interest rate cut projected for the year. Depending on which direction inflation goes, we can even see rates increase over the year. We are still early in the year so making estimates here wouldn't be helpful.

Fundamentally, ECC is aligned for success, but the external factors are slowing its growth. As long as management continues to invest in new deals that generate income, I have no concerns at the moment. Additionally, the debt maturity schedule is ideal as there are no significant maturity dates upcoming over the next two years. Only 3.7% of the underlying loan portfolio matures over the next 24 months, which means that ECC is aligned to continue collecting income from its portfolio with minimal disruptions.

Valuation

Since ECC operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the underlying value of its net assets. ECC currently trades at a very slight premium to NAV of 3.7%. Although the price trades at a small premium, I still think the valuation here is attractive when we consider that ECC has traded at an average three-year premium to NAV of 10.9%. The lower premium is likely associated with the decreasing NAV that we've seen. Looking back over its ten-year history, ECC has rarely ever touched the discount level, so I think that entry here would be beneficial to long-term holders. This is assuming that conditions eventually improve for ECC.

Although it may be several quarters away from now, future rate cuts will likely serve as a positive catalyst for growth. A lower interest rate would create a more favorable environment for both existing and new potential borrowers. Existing borrowers will see a reduced cost of debt, which would boost the quality of ECC's portfolio. At the same time, a lower cost of debt has the potential to cause an influx of new borrowers seeking capital. This would provide ECC with a greater amount of opportunities to capitalize on.

This is supported by S&P Global estimates that state a base scenario of interest rate cuts would result in a decrease in defaults to 1% by September of 2025. However, it seems like we are more aligned with the pessimistic outlook where rate cuts are going to be delayed, causing an increase in defaults up to 2.5%. These are only estimates, and we are still early in the year, so things can change over the next few months.

Since it doesn't seem like those rate cuts will happen in the short term, I am choosing to only reinvest my distributions. I will not be adding any additional capital to my position here as I do think the price can fall a bit lower. I may feel more compelled to start adding new capital once I see some improvements in the NAV and the momentum shifts to a more positive outlook. For now, I will continue to lower my cost basis through reinvestment.

Dividend Is Still Covered

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.14 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 21.5%. As previously monthly, net investment income came in at $0.24 per share for the quarter. At first glance, it may seem like the distribution is not supported by earnings, but this is not true. We have to consider that ECC is collecting a total of $0.74 per share in recurring cash flows, which fully supports the distributions and supplementals that have been paid out. ECC has rewarded shareholders with an abundance of supplemental distributions over the last two years, but I unfortunately do expect these to stop now that the margin of excess earnings has become smaller.

As it stands, I do not think that the distribution is under any threat of being reduced at the moment. However, I also don't think the current market environment caters to the possibility of future raises either. For now, I will continue to reinvest my distributions and take advantage of the discount reinvestment plan while shares trade at a slight premium. The dividend reinvestment discount can be located on page 74 of the latest annual report released back in December of 2024. I can confirm that this DRIP benefit is eligible with Fidelity. You may need to check with your brokerage to see if it supports this sort of reinvestment benefit.

Lastly, it should be noted that all distributions received from ECC are classified as ordinary income. Ordinary dividends have unfavorable tax consequences when compared to the qualified dividends you'd receive from a more traditional dividend paying stock. Therefore, a position in ECC may be best utilized within a tax advantaged account to offset any potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on ECC to a hold, as the outlook around interest rates does not seem favorable for ECC. Recurring cash flows from ECC's portfolio remain consistent, and the dividend is fully supported by earnings. Additionally, management has continually prioritized allocating capital towards new investments to keep the portfolio growth consistent. However, a higher for longer rate environment may cause there to be ongoing net realized losses and further contribute to a falling NAV. The NAV per share has now decreased for four consecutive quarters now. I will continue to reinvest my distributions but will not be adding additional capital here. I do believe that conditions and performance can eventually shift to a more positive momentum, but that time has not yet happened.

data.title: S&P 500: GDP To Fall To 0.6% - Darkening Picture Of Heightened Uncertainty
data.description:
Unfolding stagflation

My base case has been, for some time now, that the US economy would fall into a stagflation, which means higher inflation and lower growth, with a high possibility of an inflationary recession.

Note, this is the worst-case scenario for the stock market. In the stagflationary environment, the Fed is unable to cut interest as growth slows due to high inflation. Thus, the S&P 500 (SP500) could fall sharply as the stagflation unfolds, especially given the current bubble-like valuation with the PE ratio over 25. My current outlook for 2025 for the S&P500 is 4100, but this could prove to be too optimistic.

The most recent data is providing a support for the stagflationary scenario.

The University of Michigan survey

The University of Michigan consumer sentiment for February (final) has fallen sharply since January to 64.7, which was revised lower from the advance estimate and well below the expectations. This is the lowest reading over the last 12 months.

The University of Michigan inflation expectations spiked. The advanced reading showed that consumer expected a sharp spike in year ahead inflation to 4.3%. The hope was that this number could be revised lower in the final reading. However, it was not, it stayed at 4.3%.

More importantly, the 5-year inflation expectations were revised higher from the advanced reading of 3.3% to the final reading of 3.5% for February. This is extremely important, because the longer-term inflation expectations remained anchored even during the inflationary spike in 2022-23. But, based on the February reading, the long-term inflation expectations are de-anchoring, as the graph below shows.

The Fed will be worried about this number, as it frequently states that longer-term inflation expectations remain anchored. Well, this reading shows otherwise. The Fed should be thinking about increasing interest rates, despite the gloomy sentiment - that's the risk of stagflation previously discussed, as it's extremely negative for the stock market.

The S&P Global US Composite PMI

The just released S&P Global US Composite PMI flash reading for February also supports the stagflationary scenario - higher inflation with slower growth.

Particularly worrisome is the suggestion that the Service sector is actually contracting - the S&P Service PMI for February was 49.7 (below 50 is contraction) and well below the expectations of 53. This is important given that Services are 70% of US economy. In fact, this number points to a possible recession.

At the same time, S&P Global shows a spike in cost inputs, particularly for services, as the chart below shows. Again, this is extremely important knowing that the Fed is particularly worried about the service ex housing inflation.

What's behind unfolding stagflation?

As I have been warning, the Trump policies are stagflationary, particularly the tariffs, as was the reduction in Federal spending, which is likely to cause a slowing growth and eventually a recession.

The survey data, discussed in this article, is supporting my view - that exactly what consumer (in the UM survey) and businesses (in PMI survey) think it's unfolding.

So, rather than repeating my views, I will reference Chris Williamson, Chief Business Economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence, who made the following comment for the February PMI data:

The upbeat mood seen among US businesses at the start of the year has evaporated, replaced with a darkening picture of heightened uncertainty, stalling business activity and rising prices.

Optimism about the year ahead has slumped from the near-three-year highs seen at the turn of the year to one of the gloomiest since the pandemic. Companies report widespread concerns about the impact of federal government policies, ranging from spending cuts to tariffs and geopolitical developments. Sales are reportedly being hit by the uncertainty caused by the changing political landscape, and prices are rising amid tariff-related price hikes from suppliers.

Whereas the survey was indicating robust economic growth in excess of 2% late last year, the February survey signals a faltering of annualized GDP growth to just 0.6%.

While overall inflationary pressures remained muted, this reflected a squeezing of margins in the services sector as companies sought to absorb cost increases in order to offer competitive prices amid weakened demand. A concern is the sharp, tariff-related, jump in manufacturing input prices, which will likely either put further upward pressure on inflation in the coming months or further squeeze profit margins among US companies.

GDP at 0.6%?

The most interesting observation is that the recent S&P Global reading suggests that the GDP is likely to fall to 0.6% - and this is based on the current PMI survey data.

Well, this is a major surprise. The Fed expects GDP to be at 2.1% in 2025, based on the December SEP. This number would suggest that the Fed should cut more in 2025. However, longer-term inflation expectations are de-anchoring, which suggests the Fed should hike this year. It's a tough spot for the Fed - and that's the risk of stagflation.

The chart below shows that the PMI is highly correlated with GDP, so based on the recent reading, the actual data is likely to deteriorate.

Implications

The recent survey data shows that the consumer and businesses are worried about slowing growth and higher inflation - stagflation. The reasons for the "darkening picture" are the Trump policies.

With that said, the stagflationary environment can be avoided if Trump does not implement tariffs and maintains the high level of government spending, with inflows of immigrants as the labor force and consumers. That's unlikely to happen, as these were Biden policies.

The S&P500 is near the all-time highs ignoring the recent survey data - because it believes that Trump will not really push the economy into an inflationary recession. That's the hope. Obviously, that would cause a 50%+ crash in the S&P500, and the investors believe that Trump will not allow this to happen. That's the source of uncertainty. Nobody can really predict what will Trump do, but we do have the first deadline for Canada and Mexico 25% tariffs on March 4th. Let's see what happens on March 4th.

data.title: AppLovin's Culture Of Innovation: A Magnet For Growth-Oriented Investors
data.description:
When I last wrote about AppLovin Corp. (NASDAQ:APP) on November 12, 2024, it was after the stock price surged 72% after its third-quarter 2024 earnings results. I maintained my buy rating; the stock is up around 76% since I made that buy call compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) 2% rise. The company recently released fourth-quarter earnings on February 12, and the stock closed the next day up 24%.

The company beat analysts' fourth-quarter 2024 revenue and earnings estimates and issued first-quarter 2025 revenue guidance above analysts' expectations. It also displayed solid adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow growth. Investors also likely approved of the company's divestiture of its gaming studio business and the news that it was entering other markets with its Artificial Intelligence ("AI") powered ad business, raising its upside potential.

This article will discuss how the company's culture helps it maintain its revenue growth and profitability. It will also discuss AppLovin's fourth quarter results, valuation, risks, and why the stock remains on my buy list for aggressive growth investors.

AppLovin's company culture

First and foremost, the most important thing we've got here is our team and culture. Our key team continues to work very closely together to deliver on our products and our vision, and we continue to add great talent to our team while maintaining our entrepreneurial and lean culture.

Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Adam Foroughi on Fourth Quarter 2023 Earnings Call

When asked at the 51st Nasdaq conference what the company's biggest challenge is, CEO Adam Foroughi told the questioner how his role has shifted from overseeing and guiding product development to having more of a role in HR (Human Resources). He thinks the company's biggest challenge is executing his vision, and the most significant danger to executing is a cultural disruption, which leads to the churn of the company's best talent. He said the following at the NASDAQ conference:

And a lot of times, as companies grow, you end up building out these different organizations, especially HR is the culture org. And the founder loses sight of what's going on inside the organization because the company scales beyond a point of that founder having visibility. And that happened to even us. And so when I saw the opportunity to take back control of that and ensure that my biggest fear, our culture getting disrupted, and then, leading to churn of our best talent, does it ever come true, it was an opportunity that I wanted to go after. So that's what I'm focused on as far as addressing the biggest challenges that I foresee could come up into the future.

What happens at even some of the best technology companies is that as the organization grows, it can scale beyond the point of the CEO's understanding of what is happening within the company since he loses face-to-face contact with many executives as layers of bureaucracy grow between the CEO and the workers performing the tasks. Since HR maintains the organization's culture, CEO Foroughi now spends most of his time involved with HR. One of the most essential parts of the culture the CEO wants to maintain is being product-led, a business that focuses on creating products that meet customer needs. The opposite of product-led is a sales-led company whose primary focus is driving customer acquisition through sales teams. Some believe that a significant reason that companies like BlackBerry (BB) and Yahoo significantly diminished was a switch from a product-focused leadership team to a sales-oriented one.

AppLovin's culture, first and foremost, emphasizes a product-first approach, prioritizing engineering and innovation. The company's Fourth Quarter of 2024 Shareholder's Letter says the following:

In advertising, this principle [of product first] is rare. Most companies prioritize sales over engineering. At our company, the priority has always been clear: we are built to support engineering. Our success depends on their innovation and leadership, and we go where they lead.

The company's culture also emphasizes running a lean organization. One thing that can quickly kill innovation at a company is a sprawling bureaucracy as the company's revenue grows. Management at the company believes in finding ways to automate tasks before hiring employees to perform the task. If CEO Foroughi maintains this standard at the company, when the U.S. economy eventually has rough times, AppLovin should be much more resistant in needing to perform layoffs than other companies. Additionally, it should be better able to maintain profitability, even with high revenue growth rates. The Fourth Quarter Shareholder Letter states:

Many companies claim to embrace an entrepreneurial mindset, but few aim to reduce headcount even when facing growth opportunities. We've proven that small, nimble teams with a focus on automation can achieve extraordinary results.

Another thing the company stresses in its culture is nimbleness and agility. An expansion of intermediaries requiring multiple stages of sign-offs before making a decision can also be an innovation killer at maturing tech companies. Technological change is only accelerating. Companies that are slow to make decisions in an industry with rapid change can quickly fail to pivot in time to respond to changes and get disrupted. The Fourth Quarter Shareholder Letter also emphasized quick decision-making in response to change:

From the start, we anticipated that the pace of technological advancement would only accelerate. To keep up, we built our systems to be flexible with the understanding that we might need to pivot quickly and even discard past work to stay ahead. This approach remains central to our success. The launch of AXON is a testament to this philosophy, showcasing our ability to adapt and innovate rapidly.

I cannot find information on when the company released its initial version of AXON 1.0, its AI-powered ad technology. It's possible that the company released it before becoming a public company in April 2021. The first mention of Axon that I saw was when CEO Foroughi said the following on the fourth quarter 2022 earnings call:

So what are the business opportunities that we hear are most focused on today? Number one, over the last several years since we released AXON [1.0], the advancements in AI technologies have been incredible. Well, now we're working on AXON 2. We're going to use some of these new technologies for release some point in 2023.

CEO Foroughi made the above statement on February 8, 2023, only a few months after OpenAI introduced ChatGPT. However, AXON 2.0 likely doesn't use generative AI chatbot technology. Instead, AppLovin has an in-house creative agency named SparkLabs that is rapidly expanding its usage of generative AI. The "advancements in AI technologies" that he refers to are advancements in ad tech, such as UID 2.0, Advanced Contextual Analysis, Semantic AI, and several other "black box' technologies that ad tech companies pivoted to after Alphabet's Google threatened to eliminate cookies and Apple (AAPL) started allowing users to opt out of IDFA (Identifier for Advertisers) in the iOS 14.5 release on April 26, 2021. UID2.0 is the open-source identity solution The Trade Desk introduced in May 2021 and adopted by AppLovin in April 2022. Advanced Contextual Analysis is a technology that can analyze the time a person uses a device, where a person uses a device, other user behaviors, and text, images, and video that the viewer looks at to understand the context and match to relevant ads. Semantic AI uses natural language processing and machine learning to understand human language.

Much of AppLovin's success in the ad tech market is its ability to research, develop, and implement the newest technologies before competitors. One potential reason that AppLovin has been on the rise for the last several years, while competitors like Unity Software (U) have stagnated, is that these cultural advantages allow AppLovin to introduce innovative AI-powered ad technology to its customers first, producing results in the form of a higher return on investment ("ROI") for its advertising.

The proof is in the pudding. Let's look at the company's fourth-quarter and 2024 annual results.

Company fundamentals

Q4 was a major milestone, arguably our most foundational period since the AXON upgrade in 2023. For the first time, we captured meaningful holiday shopping advertising dollars and witnessed the impact of an advertising category beyond solely gaming contributing to our growth.

CEO Adam Foroughi on Fourth Quarter 2024 Earnings Call

The stock price rose sharply after the company released its fourth-quarter results; the company displayed outstanding fundamentals. However, the most significant reason investor sentiment pushed the stock much higher is the realization that AppLovin's advertising opportunity is not solely relegated to the niche mobile gaming market. CEO Foroughi said the following on the fourth quarter earnings call:

Look, our business predictably has 20% year-over-year growth, and that's just the baseline of operating within the category that we've always traditionally operated in [gaming user acquisition]. Then, you've got these things that can drive material upside that's really exciting for us [e-commerce, CTV]. They're unpredictable. And when things are unpredictable, a lot of times people assume they're going to be unpredictably bad. Well, in our business, we've got two things that could be unpredictably really good. One is these [AI] model enhancements. We're really, really early still in AI development and research both inside our company and then obviously externally too.

He refers to AI model enhancements because the company has discovered in the past that as it improves the AI algorithms, the results for its advertisers have improved, which has increased usage of its platform. So, that is one upside opportunity. Expanding its customer base is the second potentially "unpredictably really good" upside.

Historically, AppLovin limited advertisers on its platform to mobile game developers. However, the company has recently run pilot programs with e-commerce advertisers that have proven successful. Over the next year or two, management plans to gradually open the platform to a larger pool of advertisers in e-commerce, CTV, and other verticals. These new advertisers should help the company maintain compelling revenue growth for investors. So, when reviewing company guidance at the end of this section, please realize that the company's actual numbers could be higher than the company estimates if AI algorithm improvement or advertiser expansion becomes a "surprise" upside factor in this quarter or future earnings periods.

The company didn't break out the e-commerce numbers as a segment during this quarter, likely because it has been chiefly testing e-commerce. Still, management has become so confident in the growth prospects of its advertising business that CEO Foroughi announced the company was selling its Apps business.

AppLovin's fourth-quarter 2024 advertising revenue increased by 73% over the previous year's fourth quarter to $999 million. Net revenue per installation, a monetization metric, increased 49%, meaning the company made 49% more money for each user installing an app in the fourth quarter of 2024 than the previous year. App installations increased by 22%.

Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Matt Stumpf said about advertising revenue on the fourth quarter earnings call, "During the quarter, our Advertising business continued to drive increased performance for our mobile gaming partners combined with positive early results for ecommerce advertisers during the holiday season."

Its fourth-quarter advertiser adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) increased 85% over the previous year's fourth quarter to $777 million, and the Advertiser segment's adjusted EBITDA margin expanded by five points year over year to 78%.

AppLovin's fourth-quarter total revenue increased by 44% year over year to $1.37 billion, and its full-year 2024 total revenue increased by 43% over the previous year to $4.7 billion.

Although the company is GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) profitable, management still includes adjusted EBITDA figures, likely because AppLovin has many non-cash, one-time, or highly variable expenses.

AppLovin's fourth-quarter GAAP net income increased 248% to $599.20 million. Net margin expanded by 26 points to 44%. Its fourth-quarter GAAP earnings-per-share ("EPS") was $1.73, beating analysts' estimates by $0.47.

The company's stock-based compensation ("SBC") was 7.35% of quarterly revenue and around 8% of 2024 annual revenue, which is excellent for a high-growth tech company. For instance, The Trade Desk's (TTD) quarterly and annual SBC-to-revenue is 17.44% and 20.33%, respectively.

AppLovin's fourth-quarter 2024 adjusted EBITDA increased 78% to $848 million, and its fourth-quarter adjusted EBITDA margin increased 12 points to 62%. For every $1 increase in revenue, its adjusted EBITDA increased $0.89, an 89% flow-through from revenue to adjusted EBITDA. A company's "flow-through" measures the efficiency of a company's core operations. The higher the flow through, the less the company is spending to produce extra revenue growth.

The company increased fourth quarter free cash flow ("FCF") by 105% year over year to $695. It grew FCF 28% sequentially, representing 82% flow-through from adjusted EBITDA to free cash flow, which means for every $1 increase in adjusted EBITDA, its FCF increased $0.89. On the fourth quarter earnings call, CFO Stumpf said, "Free cash flow grew slightly more than our adjusted EBITDA growth over the same period due to the timing of cash tax payments."

AppLovin's trailing 12-month ("TTM") cash from operation ("CFO") to sales was 44.57%, meaning for every $1 of sales, the company generates $0.45 in CFO. Its TTM CFO was $2.099 billion, and TTM FCF was $2.094 billion, an increase of around 98% over the previous year.

At the end of the fourth quarter, AppLovin's cash and equivalents were $741.41 million, and long-term debt was $3.509 billion.

Its debt-to-equity (D/E) ratio is 3.26, which means the company finances its operations with significantly more debt than equity. The market usually considers a D/E above 2.0 a sign of a riskier company that could have problems paying its debts. The company's debt-to-EBITDA ratio is 1.21. Typically, when this ratio is below 2.0, the company has a strong debt-servicing capacity—a likely reason the market has yet to show much alarm over its high debt. Interest coverage (EBIT/interest expense) was 6.104, meaning the company is capable of paying the interest on its debt. The balance sheet should be ok if the company maintains solid operating profitability and EBITDA. Still, if operating profitability and EBITDA wanes, AppLovin could have issues paying its debt.

The midpoint of total revenue guidance, $1.37 billion, was higher than analysts' estimates of $1.32 billion before earnings. After earnings, analysts revised their revenue estimates to $1.38 billion.

The company doesn't issue EPS guidance. However, analysts raised EPS estimates from $1.31 before earnings to $1.37 after earnings.

Valuation

AppLovin's TTM price-to-sales (P/S) is 36.62, well above its three-year median of 4.951 and the Information Technology sector's median of 3.34. Some consider AppLovin's P/S valuation to be firmly in overvalued territory. However, the weakness of using TTM numbers to value a stock is that it is backward-looking and fails to account for the company's much brighter growth prospects due to its advertising addressable market expanding outside of the gaming market into e-commerce and the growing demand for its AI-powered platform.

Based on most traditional valuation metrics, the stock has a lofty valuation. Seeking Alpha's quant rates AppLovin's valuation a D+. Its valuation may be why Seeking Alpha analysts' consensus and the quant rates the stock a hold.

However, Wall Street consensus is still a Buy as I write this article. Why is that?

Let's see how AppLovin fares when looking at its forward P/S ratio versus a few ad tech peers. Based on 2025 forward revenue growth, TTM FCF, and TTM profit margin (growth, cash flow, and profitability), AppLovin outpaces The Trade Desk, Zeta Global, and Magnite, which supports a premium P/S-to-revenue growth ratio of 1.24. Based strictly on the numbers below, Wall Street analysts are likely willing to pay a premium for a company exhibiting such robust profitability and future revenue growth potential as AppLovin.

Company Name 2025 forward P/S ratio 2025 forward estimated revenue growth 2025 forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth FCF margin TTM operating margin
AppLovin 28.73 23.04% 1.24 44.40% 33.55%
Zeta Global (ZETA) 4.84 22.81% 0.21 8.7% -13.34
The Trade Desk (TTD) 13.63 18.50% 0.73 25.86% 2.62%
Magnite (MGNI) 4.47 11.21% 0.40 23.72% 16.08%

AppLovin's forward 2025 price/earnings-to-growth ("PEG") ratio is 1.91 (2025 forward P/E of 77.81 divided by analysts' estimated EPS growth of 40.72%). Its forward 2026 PEG ratio is 1.14. Generally, growth investors will allow a PEG ratio to reach 2.0 before considering a stock overvalued. Based on its 2025 PEG ratio of 1.91, some may think the stock is at fair value due to its proximity to the 2.0 level, whereas some investors may think the market overvalues the stock. However, based on its 2026 forward PEG ratio, the stock has room to run before reaching a PEG ratio of 2.0. Suppose AppLovin traded at a 2026 forward PEG ratio of 2.0; the stock price would be $869.55, a 76% rise over the February 19, 2024, stock price of $494.17. I interpret these results as the market has high expectations for AppLovin's earnings growth. As long as the company meets the market's expectations for EPS growth, the market may continue pushing the stock price higher.

Investors should remember that this valuation relies heavily on analysts' forward EPS estimates. If positive sentiment around the company turns sour, analysts could revise EPS growth estimates downwards, making the stock look overvalued.

A low FCF yield typically implies that the market has high expectations for FCF growth. Usually, the market considers a stock with an FCF yield below 1.0% a sign of overvaluation. The following chart shows that AppLovin has the lowest FCF yield at 1.22%. Some investors may be leery about investing in the stock at its current FCF yield. To maintain an FCF yield above 1.0%, AppLovin needs its FCF growth to either match or exceed its market cap growth. Suppose the company exhibits slowing FCF growth; it could provide a stiff headwind against the market cap growing much further.

My overall opinion of AppLovin's valuation is that the market has pushed the stock up to an elevated valuation. However, elevated doesn't mean overvalued. The company can potentially exceed expectations, and investors buying at the current valuation can still do well in the long term as long as such investors are risk-tolerant.

Risks

There are risks at the current valuation that could make the downside steep. Managing the company's recent rapid growth while keeping the employee headcount as low as possible may present challenges for the company by overloading existing employees or outpacing the development of automation tools. Suppose management underestimates the need for new employees. In that case, AppLovin may be unable to onboard new advertisers at a rate fast enough to capitalize on the growing demand for its ad platform. The potential drop-off in revenue growth rates could cause some investors to revalue the company downward.

AppLovin has made five acquisitions since 2020. As a result, it has $1.8 billion of goodwill, the extra amount AppLovin paid for an acquired company above its fair market value. If the fair value of the acquired business or a group of businesses falls below the value of the goodwill on the balance sheet, management must write down the goodwill, which reduces earnings. The fair value of the acquired businesses could decline for several reasons, including poor performance of the acquisitions, increased competition, technological disruption, changes in consumer behavior, and divestiture of acquired companies, in whole or in part.

Since it acquired several studios within its gaming division, management's decision to sell the unit will likely result in the company determining whether there is a goodwill impairment. Investors shouldn't be surprised if AppLovin reports some goodwill impairment soon.

Goodwill is an asset on the balance sheet, so goodwill impairment also results in asset reduction, which can adversely impact several financial ratios and rapidly change investor sentiment toward the stock from positive to negative.

The ad tech industry is highly competitive. AppLovin already faces off against several more well-known companies in the mobile ad industry, such as Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG), Meta Platforms (META), and Unity Software ((U)). As the company moves its ad business outside the gaming vertical into areas like e-commerce and Connected TV, it will face off against even more well-known competitors such as Amazon (AMZN), Adobe (ADBE), The Trade Desk, and even marketing companies with interesting AI technology like Zeta Global, which I recently discussed in this article.

Although AppLovin has impressive AI-powered technology, AXON, it will need to keep investing in it because the company faces solid competitors with sophisticated ad technology. Some of those competitors have significantly more resources and established relationships with advertisers. If the competition slows AppLovin's revenue growth or squeezes its margins, Wall Street may become unwilling to support the stock at its current valuation.

AppLovin remains a long-term buy

The market has priced in significant revenue, earnings, and FCF growth expectations into AppLovin's stock price. Suppose it misses analysts' estimates or something is perceived to threaten its future growth opportunities. In that case, the stock price has substantial downside potential, a reason risk-averse investors should avoid investing in it at the current valuation.

However, there are reasons growth investors remain interested in this company. Until recently, AppLovin's ad business was mostly relegated to user acquisition in the mobile gaming market, which some estimate to have a total addressable market ("TAM") size anywhere from $40 billion to $65 billion. Within the last year, the company announced its intention to enter the Direct-to-Consumer (D2C) e-commerce market. Statista estimated that the U.S. D2C online market was $213 billion at the end of 2024. Examples of D2C companies include Hims & Hers (HIMS), Dollar Shave Club, Blue Apron, and Warby Parker. The company also has entered the Connected TV ("CTV") ad market, which Statista estimated at around $29 billion in 2024. Management has also hinted at entering other advertising markets, potentially further increasing its TAM beyond e-commerce, CTV, and mobile gaming.

At the 51st Nasdaq conference, CEO Foroughi said the following:

We now have the possibility of going after the whole economy that's connected through digital and creating more discovery. And if we're able to execute on that, we think we can be a company that creates economies. And when you think about the biggest companies in the world, Facebook, Amazon, Google, they've created economies. The biggest companies either enable the creation or do it directly. And that's what we aspire to do.

In the above statement, he implies that AppLovin can become a force in the digital economy on the level of Facebook, Amazon, and Google. That's quite a bold statement, and I am unsure whether it can reach those levels. However, the stock has a significant potential upside if it can execute and grab market share in the D2C e-commerce and CTV advertising markets. Aggressive growth investors who understand the risks and are willing to withstand the potential high volatility in the stock price should consider purchasing a few shares. I maintain my buy rating on AppLovin for aggressive growth investors only.

data.title: Dollar General: Business Economics Worsen, But Low Valuation Limits Risk (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I covered Dollar General (NYSE:DG) with a bearish outlook a year ago in "Dollar General: Business Model Struggling In Modern America." I expected the stock would lose value due to increased competition from online retailers like Amazon (AMZN) and Temu and larger retailers like Costco (COST), while its focus on low-income areas exposed to added economic, labor, and theft-related issues. I disagreed with the analyst consensus that its EPS would rise, seeing that it would likely face increased profit margin pressures in 2024.

Since then, Dollar General's TTM operating margins have collapsed to 4% despite stable sales. The stock has lost 45% of its value and is now at $76, below my low-end price target range of $90. Its forward "P/E" is currently 13X, above my target range of 10-12.5X, indicating that the company's EPS outlook has declined substantially.

Expectations have declined similarly, with analysts now looking to an EPS of around $5.9 this year, potentially rising back to $6.5 by 2027. That said, the future earnings forecast for Dollar General has declined markedly. See below:

The analyst consensus still looks toward an eventual recovery, though the forecast has shifted sharply lower since I covered it last. Analysts look to recover to an EPS of ~$10 by 2029-2030, meaning they expect it to take close to a decade for its income to normalize from its past peak. Of course, if it can manage that recovery, Dollar General's long-term forward "P/E" today may be pretty low at ~7X; however, that requires an uncertain recovery. Short interest on DG is slightly elevated at 5.9%, indicating some speculators are betting that it will face more significant woes. Thus, it appears to be an excellent time to take an updated look at the company and its future potential.

Does Dollar General Have a Niche?

My previous view on Dollar General primarily focused on the failure of its business model in the modern economy. In short, the company is facing a "death from a thousand cuts." I pointed to poor pricing power, labor shortage costs, theft issues, and competition as numerous negative factors impacting its profit margins. Some commenters disagreed with my view that it faces competition from online discount retailers like Temu or in-store retailers like Walmart or Costco.

Regarding Temu, though many DG customers are likely looking for immediate products, that is not necessarily true for all DG customers. Analytics surveys show that Dollar General customers who use Temu are spending more on that platform. Dollar General's market share in discount spending has also declined following the launch of Temu. Temu has a distinct pricing power edge on most of its goods compared to both Amazon and Dollar General. That isn't to say anything about quality, but Dollar General customers are likely not looking for quality regardless. Again, I'm not saying all or most DG customers are competing with Temu, but if 10-20% are, then that is sufficient to create a pricing power strain at Dollar General.

The situation with Walmart and Costco is relatively clear-cut. Of course, Dollar General and Walmart often operate in different locations and have different niches. However, Walmart has tremendously better prices per volume than Dollar General. Many Dollar General shoppers may care more about total cost than actual value, but in an inflationary economy, I expect that value price sensitivity will grow. Dollar General's prices are essentially a non-starter, particularly when looking at groceries. Now that many people use delivery apps for groceries, Dollar General lacks both a pricing and location benefit edge.

Further, because its prices are higher, I see no reason why DG demand should be anti-cyclical, and evidence today suggests that it's failing because people are becoming more price-sensitive. This cyclical exposure is likely to increase if inflation rebounds because Dollar General's business model has a negative scale to volume. For example, if input costs grow (packaging, wages, transportation, etc.), that will generally push costs up more (as a percentage) for low-volume goods than high-volume ones (such as those in Costco).

Those factors saw a fair amount of disagreement among commenters in my previous article. I do not think that, individually, competitive factors or poor business economics will cause DG to fail, but combined, they may. If Dollar General loses a small amount of pricing power to online discount retailers while another small portion shifts to Walmart or Costco (easier with delivery apps), the discount retailers' place in the modern economy is limited. The data clearly shows that it faces increased competition from online retailers and bulk in-store retailers. The trend in its sales and margins also indicates that it has negative economic exposure, with that risk increasing with inflation. Perhaps Dollar General was a counter-cyclical with limited competitive pressure a decade or two ago, but if that were true now, we would not be seeing its margins collapse.

The one issue I did not see much commenter contention on is Dollar General's exposure to theft. Looking at its 2024 earnings, the company pointed to gross margins declining due to "Increased markdowns, increased inventory damages, a greater proportion of sales coming from the consumables category, and increased shrink." Although the company does not publish its shrink rate, efforts to lower it by limiting self-checkout in 2024 contributed to a 29 bps margin improvement. Since it managed a 29 bps margin improvement by reducing shrink, its shrink issue is likely quite significant and the company has a ways to go.

I believe theft is a significant issue for DG but is secondary to its poor business economics. Indeed, its high theft rate is essentially the result of its lousy business economics by positioning itself in lower-income areas. As it shifts its stores toward rural areas, I expect the shrink issue will come down, but it should persist until we see theft decline across the US.

What is Dollar General Worth Today?

Dollar General's business economics are poor, and I expect this will exacerbate pressures on its profit margins. The company is making some changes to turn itself around, such as removing self-checkouts, remodeling stores, focusing on rural store openings, and offering same-day delivery services. That said, until I see its profit margins improve, I doubt these changes will secure its position. We see many signs that inflation is rebounding today, potentially exacerbating its broader economic weakness.

Although Dollar General's position is, to me, very poor, the stock's valuation is extremely low. Last year, I was among the most bearish analysts on DG, but its current price is below the expected target range of $90-$110. The company's liquidity and solvency position is also relatively healthy. Its debt-to-EBITDA has risen due to its lower income but is a reasonable 2.3X today. Its working capital is positive, while its cash and short-term investments total around $530M. Its inventories are slightly elevated, but not as much as in 2023. See below:

Although Dollar General's profit margin trend may not improve, it's a long way away from serious financial issues. The company has sufficient liquidity and solvency to avoid financial woes for some time and should be able to raise external cash if need be. Of course, its cash flows are also quite strong, with an operating cash flow of about $3.15B TTM, giving it ample free cash flow despite higher CapEx spending of $1.5B. Most of the remaining cash is distributed to investors via dividends, share repurchases (which have slowed), and potential debt reduction (which we're not currently seeing). See below:

Over the coming years, I expect Dollar General to continue closing underperforming stores in urban areas and shifting toward rural markets. However, that may not be sufficient to secure a long-term stable position, as rural markets still face poor business economic trends. That said, Dollar General is not in financial straits and has the cash flow to navigate rough waters for an extended period.

Dollar Tree (DLTR) and Dollar General have seen their operating margins crash. Dollar Tree's margins are worse as it took significant one-time losses in closing underperforming stores. If we look at the trend since 2019, it seems that DG's operating margins are falling 0.5% to 1% per year, potentially at an accelerated pace:

If this continues, Dollar General may not earn an operating income by the end of the decade or possibly in the middle of the next decade. I believe this negative trend will likely accelerate over the coming years. I also expect inflationary supply-side pressures to rebound this year, likely exacerbating Dollar General's poor business economics. That said, my view is speculative and partially dependent on the impact of tariffs and the monetary system, which are indirectly related to Dollar General. I think supply-side inflation should worsen Dollar General's position compared to non-discount store competitors. Still, it is uncertain if supply-side inflation will rebound as much as I expect.

The Bottom Line

My outlook on DG is neutral, upgrading it from my previous view. I think the company's business model is essentially untenable in the modern economy. Still, it should retain profitability for some time if it continues to reduce exposure to underperforming areas and products. The company should continue to have strong positive cash flows, securing its financial stability over the next five to ten years. Considering that I believe its valuation is generally low, it could be undervalued if its income rebounds as much as most analysts currently expect.

Still, although DG's valuation is low, I would avoid the stock as it seems to be a value trap from a long-term standpoint. In my opinion, the best its managers can do is prolong its life, but at this point, I do not think the company will exist in its current form 10-15 years from now. The dollar-store niche seems untenable as people can deliver groceries cheaply using apps and buy knick-knacks cheaply on Temu.

People will likely continue to shop at Dollar General for years. Still, I expect fewer customers, which will cause Dollar General to continue taking losses and closing underperforming stores. Although I think its long-term viability is doubtful, at its current valuation, DG's future cash flows may be more than its current market capitalization. Thus, there is not a strong argument to be outright bearish on DG today, and it could be undervalued if its managers create a plan to secure a more viable niche.

data.title: Banco Santander: Starting To Get Its Due From The Market
data.description:
Shares of Banco Santander (NYSE:SAN) have been a strong performer since my last update in October, with robust fourth quarter results and bullish FY2025 guidance powering a ~26% total return for the ADSs.

The market has long been skeptical of the merits of this bank's sprawling geographical footprint, possibly driving a 'conglomerate discount' for the stock. Discounted multiples are often seen as a bad thing. They aren't, at least for long-term investors: the combination of an ongoing high yield and growth is typically better for wealth creation than the short-term boost from multiple expansion. This basically formed the core of my 'Buy' argument here, with Santander offering investors a ~10% shareholder yield on a modest 50% payout ratio.

That said, the market has re-rated these shares since my last piece, leaving them at a premium to tangible book value. Although that means the valuation is richer than it was 3-4 months ago, the outlook for earnings and capital returns remains attractive. Accordingly, I'm leaving my 'Buy' rating unchanged.

Earnings Remain Strong, With Potential Upside From Guidance

Santander delivered another set of record earnings in its fourth quarter, with profit attributable to ordinary shareholders rising 11% year-on-year to €3.265 billion, beating consensus by around 12%. This propelled full-year 2024 net income to €12.57 billion, beating by around €350 million, and marked the bank's third straight year of record earnings. FY2024 net income also mapped to a strong return on tangible equity of 16.3%, up 120bps from 2023.

Excluding the corporate center, Santander's Retail & Commercial Banking ('RCB') segment contributed roughly half of group-wide net income last year, delivering a ~19% return on tangible equity. This segment can be further broken down into numerous discrete geographical businesses, including Spain (~26% of segmental pre-tax income), the United Kingdom (~15%), Brazil (~12%) and Mexico (~12%).

Santander's sprawling geographic footprint has its skeptics, but it does help smooth out earnings volatility for the bank. For instance, its Spanish business is now seeing net interest income ('NII') roll over, as lower Eurozone interest rates are leading to contracting deposit margins there. Santander was paying just 75bps to its Spanish depositors last quarter. A strong deposit franchise like this is a major asset when interest rates are rising, but it also means the bank has less scope to manage margins when rates are falling. This, however, was offset by growth elsewhere, and total RCB NII advanced by around 5% sequentially last quarter to €7.3 billion.

Furthermore, the geographical complexity of this business doesn't lead to weak efficiency like you might expect. On the contrary, RCB delivered a stellar cost/income ratio of 39.4% last year, a 340bps improvement on 2023.

Santander is seeing particularly good growth in fee income – a welcome development for investors.
